name.en-US: Commentary on the De Generatione et Corruptione
name.la: Sententia super De Generatione et Corruptione

description.en-US: Aquinas's commentary on Aristotle's *De Generatione et Corruptione* — on substantial change, the elements, and prime matter.
description.la: Expositio *De Generatione et Corruptione* Aristotelis — de mutatione substantiali, de elementis, de materia prima.

sources.description: English translation by Pierre H. Conway OP and R. F. Larcher OP (1964, typescript), revised by the Aquinas Institute. Mirrored from aquinas.cc.
Latin text from the Leonine / Marietti editions (public domain), mirrored from aquinas.cc.

title.en-US: Book 1 — Prologue
Prologue — Prologue
Book 2
Prologue
Lecture 1 — Aristotle's Preface. Various previous opinions on the difference between generation and alteration.
Lecture 2 — The basic reason for these differing opinions on generation and alteration.
Lecture 3 — What must be treated. Opinions of Democritus and Leucippus.
Lecture 4 — Democritus' argument that natural bodies are composed out of indivisible bodies.
Lecture 5 — Resolution of Democritus' argument
Lecture 6 — Does simple generation exist? Problem and solution.
Lecture 7 — The cause on the part of matter why generation never fails.
Lecture 8 — Why, in mutual generation and corruption, there, is sometimes absolute generation and qualified corruption, and conversely.
Lecture 9 — The cause of the difference between absolute and qualified generation in things not reciprocally generated.
Lecture 10 — The difference between generation and alteration.
Lecture 11 — Growth differs from generation and alteration both as to subject and to manner.
Lecture 12 — The subject of growth is not something incorporeal or lacking size.
Lecture 13 — Matter, even as conceived by Platonists, cannot lack size and be the subject of growth.
Lecture 14 — Problems on the nature of that by which something grows.
Lecture 15 — Solution of the difficulty proposed in the previous lecture.
Lecture 16 — How growth takes place. Its difference from generation.
Lecture 17 — Comparison of growth, to food. How diminution occurs.
Lecture 18 — Continuation of Commentary by an Anonymous Commentator
Book 3
Prologue
